statements.statement: Class I lasers are always low power and safe to look at
statements.explanation: Class I systems may contain Class IV sources that are fully enclosed. The classification is based on accessible emission during operation, not internal laser power.

statements.statement: Class 2 lasers rely on the blink reflex for protection
statements.explanation: Correct! Class II (<1mW visible) is designed to be safe because the blink reflex limits exposure to about 0.25 seconds.

statements.statement: Class 3R means 'relatively safe' for intentional viewing
statements.explanation: 3R is potentially hazardous - direct viewing can exceed MPE. The 'R' stands for Reduced requirements, not Reduced risk.

statements.statement: Class IV is only dangerous with direct beam exposure
statements.explanation: Class IV lasers can cause injury from scattered/diffuse reflections, skin exposure, and can ignite materials.

classification_pyramid.title: FDA Laser Class Pyramid
classification_pyramid.description: Click each level to explore the characteristics of each FDA laser class

details.accessible_emission: Cannot exceed Class I AEL during operation
details.key_point: Class I products may contain high-power lasers that are completely enclosed

levels.description: Visible lasers, low power, not intended for prolonged viewing

details.accessible_emission: Visible only, specific time limits apply
details.key_point: Requires warning statement but not full logotype
Protection relies on blink reflex - deliberate staring can cause injury

levels.description: Visible lasers 1-5mW, hazardous with intentional viewing

details.key_point: CAUTION label required; can cause eye damage if viewed directly

details.accessible_emission: 5-500mW visible; various limits for UV and IR
details.hazard_potential: High - direct viewing causes injury; skin hazard at higher powers
details.key_point: DANGER label required; remote interlock connector required

levels.description: Highest hazard - diffuse reflection can injure

details.hazard_potential: Very High - diffuse reflections, skin burns, fire hazard
details.key_point: Most stringent controls required; manual reset required (post-1986)

comparison_table.title: FDA vs IEC Classification Comparison
comparison_table.description: Understanding the relationship between FDA and IEC classifications

comparison_data.differences: Generally aligned; FDA has specific measurement parameters
FDA-specific class for limited viewing time
IIIa limited to 5mW visible; 3R has different AELs
Similar hazard levels; some AEL differences

ael_concept.title: What are Accessible Emission Limits?
ael_concept.analogy: Think of AELs as speed limits for laser radiation. Just as speed limits keep traffic safe, AELs keep laser products within safe emission levels for their class.
key_points: AELs define the maximum radiation that can be accessible during operation
Classification is based on the HIGHEST level of accessible radiation
Measurement must use worst-case conditions (maximum emission, closest access)
Different AELs apply for different wavelengths and exposure durations

measurement_parameters.title: FDA Measurement Parameters (21 CFR 1040.10(e))
measurement_parameters.description: Click each parameter to understand how FDA requires measurements to be made

parameters.name: Aperture
parameters.purpose: Simulates dilated pupil - worst-case eye access

parameters.name: Solid Angle
parameters.purpose: Defines the acceptance cone for measurement

wavelength_regions.title: Wavelength-Dependent AELs
wavelength_regions.description: AELs vary significantly by wavelength region

regions.special_factors: k1 and k2 wavelength correction factors apply
Blink reflex protection for Class II

calculation_walkthrough.title: AEL Calculation Example
calculation_walkthrough.scenario: Calculate the Class I AEL for a 632.8nm HeNe laser with 0.1 second exposure

steps.title: Determine Applicable Formula
steps.content: For visible, t between 18μs and 10s: MPE = 1.8 × t^0.75 mW/cm²

steps.title: Apply Aperture Area
steps.content: Class I AEL = MPE × Area = 0.32 mW/cm² × 0.385 cm² = 0.123 mW

classification_wizard.description: Walk through the classification process step by step

cases.name: The CD Player
cases.question: What is the FDA classification?
cases.explanation: Class I - The high-power laser is fully enclosed; no hazardous radiation is accessible during operation.

cases.name: The Presentation Pointer
cases.question: What is the FDA classification?
cases.explanation: Class IIIa - Visible laser between 1-5mW falls into Class IIIa. The total collectable radiant power is the determining factor.

cases.name: The Engraving System

specifications.interlocks: Door interlocks prevent operation when open

cases.question: What is the FDA classification?
cases.explanation: Class IV - 50W far exceeds Class IIIb limits. Even with interlocks, the accessible emission level determines classification.

requirements_drag_drop.title: Match Requirements to Laser Class
requirements_drag_drop.instructions: Drag each requirement to all laser classes where it applies

labels.description: Yellow warning triangle with 'LASER RADIATION' text
labels.required_text: CAUTION - LASER RADIATION - DO NOT STARE INTO BEAM

labels.condition: CAUTION if irradiance ≤ 2.5 mW/cm², otherwise DANGER

labels.description: Red warning triangle with 'LASER RADIATION' text
labels.required_text: DANGER - LASER RADIATION - AVOID DIRECT EYE EXPOSURE
labels.additional_requirements: Aperture labels required at all emission points

questions.scenario: A laser product emits 3.5 mW at 650 nm continuously. The total collectable radiant power through a 7mm aperture is 3.2 mW.
questions.question: What is the FDA classification?
questions.explanation: Visible laser with total collectable radiant power between 1-5mW is Class IIIa. The 3.2 mW measurement determines the class.

questions.scenario: You are designing a Class IIIb laser system.
questions.question: Which of the following are NOT required for Class IIIb?
questions.explanation: Manual reset is only required for Class IV (post-1986). All other options are required for Class IIIb.
